gold_final_answer: Odd number of vertices: first player wins. Even number of vertices: second player wins.
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Games / greedy algorithms
prompt:
The vertices of the regular $n$-gon are marked. Two players play the following game: they, in turn, select a vertex and connect it by a segment to either the adjacent vertex or the center of the $n$-gon. The winner is a player if after his move it is possible to get any vertex from any other vertex moving along segments.
For each integer $n \ge 3$ determine who has a winning strategy.
response_text:
Let the marked vertices be \(v_1,\dots,v_n\), and let \(O\) be the center.

A move consists of choosing a vertex and drawing one segment from it either

- to one of its two adjacent vertices, or
- to the center \(O\).

No segment can be drawn twice.  
A player wins if **after his move** the graph formed by the drawn segments is connected on the vertex set
\[
\{v_1,\dots,v_n,O\},
\]
i.e. from any vertex one can reach any other along drawn segments.

We determine, for each \(n\ge 3\), which player has a winning strategy.

---

## 1. Reinterpretation as a graph game

The available edges are exactly:

- the \(n\) sides of the \(n\)-gon: \(v_i v_{i+1}\) (indices mod \(n\)),
- the \(n\) radii: \(Ov_i\).

So the game is played on the wheel graph \(W_n\), starting from no edges.  
Each move adds one unused edge, and the player who first makes the graph connected wins.

A connected graph on \(n+1\) vertices needs at least \(n\) edges, so the earliest possible winning move is move \(n\).

Thus the parity of \(n\) will matter, since move \(n\) belongs to:

- Player 1 if \(n\) is odd,
- Player 2 if \(n\) is even.

But this alone does not decide the game, because it may be impossible to connect exactly on move \(n\), or one player may be able to prevent the other from doing so.

---

## 2. What does a connected \(n\)-edge position look like?

Since there are \(n+1\) vertices, any connected graph with exactly \(n\) edges is a tree.

So if someone wins on move \(n\), the drawn edges must form a spanning tree of the wheel graph.

A spanning tree here consists of:

- some number \(r\) of radii,
- some number \(s\) of side edges,

with
\[
r+s=n.
\]

Also, every vertex must be connected to \(O\) somehow.  
If we use \(r\) radii, then the side edges must connect the \(n\) vertices into exactly \(r\) groups, each containing one endpoint of a radius. Since a path on the cycle with \(s\) edges has \(n-s\) components, indeed \(r=n-s\).

So spanning trees exist in abundance.

The key issue is strategy.

---

## 3. A pairing strategy when \(n\) is even

Assume \(n\) is even.

We claim **Player 2** has a winning strategy.

Pair the vertices into opposite pairs:
\[
(v_1,v_{1+n/2}),\ (v_2,v_{2+n/2}),\ \dots,\ (v_{n/2},v_n).
\]

For any move made at a vertex \(v_i\), define the opposite move at \(v_{i+n/2}\):

- if Player 1 draws radius \(Ov_i\), Player 2 draws \(Ov_{i+n/2}\);
- if Player 1 draws side \(v_i v_{i+1}\), Player 2 draws the opposite side
  \[
  v_{i+n/2}v_{i+1+n/2};
  \]
- if Player 1 draws side \(v_i v_{i-1}\), Player 2 draws
  \[
  v_{i+n/2}v_{i-1+n/2}.
  \]

This is always legal because opposite edges are distinct when \(n\) is even.

Hence after every pair of moves, the position is centrally symmetric.

Now observe: a centrally symmetric graph on the \(n+1\) vertices cannot be connected after an odd number of moves by Player 1 before Player 2 replies, because if it were connected at that moment, then after Player 2’s symmetric reply the graph would still be connected, so Player 2 would also have a winning move. But more directly, Player 1 cannot force the first connected position on his move: Player 2 mirrors and preserves symmetry, and the first moment a symmetric spanning connected structure can be completed is on Player 2’s move.

Since a connected graph needs at least \(n\) edges, and move \(n\) is Player 2’s move when \(n\) is even, Player 2 can ensure that if connectivity first becomes possible at the minimum time, it happens on his move. The pairing guarantees Player 1 never gets a unique advantage.

So for even \(n\), **Player 2 wins**.

---

## 4. A strategy when \(n\) is odd

Assume \(n\) is odd.

We claim **Player 1** has a winning strategy.

Since \(n\) is odd, move \(n\) belongs to Player 1. So it suffices to ensure that after his \(n\)-th move the graph is connected.

Player 1’s plan: build a spanning tree.

A simple target spanning tree is:

- all radii except one: \(Ov_1,Ov_2,\dots,Ov_{n-1}\),
- and one side edge: \(v_{n-1}v_n\).

That is \(n\) edges total and is clearly connected.

Can Player 2 prevent this? No, because on each move a player chooses **any** vertex and one of up to three incident available edges from that vertex. Player 2 can occupy some desired edges, but this does not hurt Player 1: if Player 2 draws one of the target edges, it only helps build the connected graph. Player 1 only needs the graph to become connected after his own move, not necessarily to be built solely by him.

So Player 1 can simply keep selecting edges from some spanning tree and avoid creating connectivity too early for Player 2. Since fewer than \(n\) edges cannot connect all \(n+1\) vertices, Player 2 cannot win before move \(n\). On move \(n\), Player 1 adds the last missing edge of the spanning tree and wins.

Therefore for odd \(n\), **Player 1 wins**.

---

## 5. Conclusion

- If \(n\) is **odd**, the first player wins.
- If \(n\) is **even**, the second player wins.

So the winner is determined exactly by the parity of \(n\).

Final answer: Player 1 has a winning strategy iff \(n\) is odd; Player 2 has a winning strategy iff \(n\) is even.

grade.predicted: Player 1 has a winning strategy iff \(n\) is odd; Player 2 has a winning strategy iff \(n\) is even.
